perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy. If, in the absence of a protective order, the recipient determines that it is required to disclose such information, it may disclose without breach of this Agreement only Confidential Inform ation specifically required and only to the extent compelled to do so . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text:
a. Law that applies; jurisdiction and venue. This Agreement has been negotiated and executed in the State of California and shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of California, without reference to conflicts of law provisions. In the event of any legal action to enforce or i nterpret this Agreement, the sole and exclusive venue shall be a court of competent jurisdiction located in Orange County, California, and the Parties hereto agree to and do hereby submit to the jurisdiction of such court, notwithstanding California Code o f Civil Procedure section 394. Furthermore, the Parties specifically agree to waive any and all rights to request that an action be transferred for trial to another county.
Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy. If, in the absence of a protective order, the recipient determines that it is required to disclose such information, it may disclose without breach of this Agreement only Confidential Inform ation specifically required and only to the extent compelled to do so . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure regarding compliance with the California Public Records Act into the 'Law that applies; jurisdiction and venue' section obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed, understood, and followed by Microsoft. This could lead to violations of the CPRA.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Government Code Section 6253 mandates that public records are open to inspection at all times during the office hours of the state or local agency and every person has a right to inspect any record. By burying the disclosure related to CPRA within a section primarily discussing jurisdiction and venue, the contract reduces emphasis that both parties must be aware of this law and its effect. This relocation also potentially hinders Microsoft's understanding of its responsibilities under CPRA, as it makes it less prominent than it would be in a dedicated section about confidential information disclosure, potentially violating the spirit of the CPRA, which emphasizes transparency and public access.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The perturbation type is 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction', which applies when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The original contract clearly states the County's responsibilities under the California Public Records Act (CPRA) in a section that directly addresses confidential information disclosure. By moving the disclosure regarding CPRA into a section primarily dealing with jurisdiction and venue, the changed text obscures the importance of the CPRA and potentially hinders Microsoft's understanding of its responsibilities under the CPRA, especially regarding disclosing confidential information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Government Code Section 6253 (as mentioned in the Law Explanation), implies the importance of public access to records. Burying the CPRA disclosure reduces emphasis and prominence, thus contradicting the spirit of transparency which emphasizes public access.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us will comply with all export laws that apply to Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: f. Enforceability. If any provision of this Agreement is unenforceable, the parties (or, if we cannot agree, a court) will revise it so that it can be enforced. Even if no revision is possible, the rest of this Agreement will remain in place. Each of us will comply with all export laws that apply to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the compliance with export laws clause into the 'Enforceability' section reduces the visibility and emphasis on the need to comply with such laws. This could lead to unintentional violations of export regulations, as it is not prominently placed within the core obligations related to confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR, specifically 15 CFR § 732.1, outlines the steps for determining export control requirements. By embedding the clause about complying with export laws within a section about the enforceability of the contract, the relocation downplays the affirmative obligation to actively determine and adhere to export control requirements, potentially violating the EAR by not ensuring proper compliance measures are prominently considered and executed by both parties regarding confidential information that may be subject to export controls. This increases the risk of unintentional violations, as the party is not strongly reminded to seek this information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the export law compliance clause into the 'Enforceability' section, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, diminishes its prominence and impact. This is significant because, as the Law Explanation states, it downplays the affirmative obligation to actively determine and adhere to export control requirements. The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 2, are designed to ensure compliance. By embedding the clause within a less visible section, the contract increases the risk of unintentional violations, as the party is not strongly reminded to seek this information. This falls under Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction because a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. Therefore, it is a significant enough contradiction to warrant a YES.

perturbation.original_text: The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.changed_text: b. Compliance with law. Each of us will comply with all export laws that apply to Confidential Information. The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause related to the duration of confidentiality obligations included an important caveat that if other laws require a longer period of confidentiality, then that longer period applies. By moving this critical information into the section regarding 'Compliance with Law', it diminishes the prominence of this consideration for the main duration clause, Section 4. Because most parties would read the sections in order, it would be easy to miss or overlook this vital exception, which could potentially result in the parties unknowingly violating laws that impose longer confidentiality periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426 codifies the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). While CUTSA does not explicitly dictate a 'confidentiality period,' the misappropriation of a trade secret can lead to ongoing damages. By shifting the disclosure that some laws might require a longer confidentiality period into another section, the NDA obscures the fact that the standard five-year limitation may not be adequate, which may leave the trade secret holder without the legal protection to stop future harm. This conflicts with the spirit and intent of CUTSA, which seeks to protect trade secrets against misappropriation and damage.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. The original text clearly states that the five-year confidentiality period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period. The changed text moves this critical caveat into a section regarding 'Compliance with Law,' diminishing its prominence and potentially leading parties to overlook this vital exception. This contradicts the intent of CUTSA, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which seeks to protect trade secrets against misappropriation and damage, including future harm, which may be indefinite. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Civil Code § 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating the legal basis for protecting trade secrets.
